mnemonics.title: Kruskal's Steps
mnemonics.content: SORT, THEN REPORT: Sort edges by weight, then repeatedly check if edge connects different components using Find, add if safe (Union), stop when V-1 edges selected.

mnemonics.title: Why No Cycles
mnemonics.content: FIND before UNION: Before adding edge, Find if vertices are in same set. If same = cycle (skip), if different = safe (Union and add).

acronyms.usage: Data structure used to maintain collection of disjoint sets, enabling efficient cycle detection in Kruskal's Algorithm.
The output of Kruskal's Algorithm - a tree connecting all vertices with minimum total edge weight.
Alternative name for DSU - supports Make-set, Find, and Union operations in nearly constant time.

visualTips.visualization: Think of sets as circles containing vertices. Union is pouring one circle into another. Find asks 'which circle does this vertex belong to?' Path compression flattens nested circles into a star pattern.
Imagine edges as ropes of varying lengths (weights). Kruskal's picks shortest ropes first, but only connects ropes that don't create loops - like building a bridge network with minimal rope length.
Use two fingers to trace paths from each vertex. If they meet at any point, vertices are connected; adding another edge creates a loop.

analogies.analogy: Kruskal's is like building roads between cities by always choosing the cheapest road that doesn't create a detour (cycle). You stop when all cities are connected.
Think of MST construction as a spider web growing. Kruskal's adds strands one by one from outside, connecting different threads. Prim's starts from center and grows outward.
Each person starts in their own circle. When two people become friends (edge added), their circles merge. If already in same circle, becoming 'friends' again would create redundancy (cycle).
